jokes.text: Maybe it was just the cat. / No, she's sleeping with me. [Cat meows]
jokes.setup: Bart suggests mysterious sounds might be the cat
jokes.punchline: Homer's literal interpretation reveals he sleeps with the cat
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer literal-mindedness subverts Bart's reasonable explanation. Strong character integration and efficient misdirection.

jokes.text: Attic? Oh, that's silly. [Laughing loudly] / Seriously, though, don't ever go up there.
jokes.setup: Homer dismisses the attic concern with exaggerated laughter
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts himself with serious warning
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup-punchline structure with Homer's typical obliviousness to his own contradictions.

jokes.text: I work my butt off to feed you four kids / What? / Three. We have three kids, Homer. / Yeah. Three nosy kids.
jokes.setup: Homer claims to work hard for four kids
jokes.punchline: Marge corrects him; Homer doubles down on 'three nosy kids' confirming he forgot about Hugo
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer mathematical confusion that reveals plot information while staying in character.

jokes.text: [Gasps] The unsold copies of Dad's autobiography.
jokes.setup: Kids discover something shocking in the attic
jokes.punchline: It's just Homer's failed book, suggesting it was so bad it's genuinely frightening
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection that uses Homer's established incompetence for a visual gag.

jokes.text: See, Marge? Who needs a car wash when you can just drive around in the rain?
jokes.setup: Family returns home in what appears to be a rainstorm
jokes.punchline: Homer's typically backwards logic about car maintenance
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer cheapskate logic delivered with characteristic confidence.

jokes.text: I believe they prefer to be called 'conjoined twins.' / And hillbillies prefer to be called 'sons of the soil,' but it ain't gonna happen.
jokes.setup: Dr. Hibbert offers politically correct terminology
jokes.punchline: Homer's crude analogy dismisses political correctness entirely
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-driven humor that skewers both political correctness and Homer's stubbornness with creative analogy.

jokes.text: Too crazy for Boys Town, too much of a boy for Crazy Town.
jokes.setup: Dr. Hibbert explains Hugo's institutional rejection
jokes.punchline: Perfectly parallel phrasing about fictional incompatible institutions
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay with perfect parallel structure. Highly quotable and demonstrates excellent comedic timing.

jokes.text: We chained Hugo up in the attic like an animal and fed him a bucket of fish heads once a week. It saved our marriage.
jokes.setup: Dr. Hibbert describes horrific treatment of Hugo
jokes.punchline: Casual mention that this abuse somehow helped Homer and Marge's relationship
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered with medical professional detachment. The casual marriage counseling angle is brilliantly absurd.

jokes.text: We'll search out every place a sick, twisted, solitary misfit might run to. I'll start with Radio Shack.
jokes.setup: Homer describes where Hugo might hide
jokes.punchline: Radio Shack as the first destination for misfits
jokes.explanation: Clever pop culture observation about Radio Shack's customer base delivered with Homer's matter-of-fact tone.

jokes.text: You're crazy. / Am I? Well, perhaps we're all a little crazy. I know I am.
jokes.setup: Bart accuses Hugo of being crazy
jokes.punchline: Hugo's philosophical response immediately undermines itself with self-admission
jokes.explanation: Classic 'am I crazy?' trope with Hugo immediately confirming Bart's assessment.

jokes.text: Look. I've been practicing. I made a pigeon-rat.
jokes.setup: Hugo claims surgical reunion will be easy
jokes.punchline: Evidence is a grotesque hybrid animal
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd visual gag that demonstrates Hugo's twisted competence while being inherently funny.

jokes.text: Hugo's scar is on the wrong side. He couldn't have been the evil, left twin. That means the evil twin is and always has been... Bart.
jokes.setup: Dr. Hibbert notices Hugo's scar placement
jokes.punchline: Revelation that Bart was actually the evil twin all along
jokes.explanation: Brilliant plot twist that recontextualizes the entire segment. Perfect misdirection that makes sense of Bart's established character.

jokes.text: Oh, don't look so shocked. W-Well, chalk this one up to carelessness on my part.
jokes.setup: Family reacts to revelation about Bart being the evil twin
jokes.punchline: Dr. Hibbert casually dismisses decades of psychological trauma as a minor error
jokes.explanation: Dr. Hibbert's characteristic professional understatement applied to a massive psychological mistake.

jokes.text: Mom, Hugo's eating his napkin. [All laughing] He's so cute.
jokes.setup: Family dinner with Hugo now integrated
jokes.punchline: Hugo's feral behavior treated as endearing family quirk
jokes.explanation: Sweet resolution joke showing the family's unconditional acceptance of Hugo's oddities.

jokes.text: Oh, you finish your fish heads, then we'll talk.
jokes.setup: Bart asks for turkey at family dinner
jokes.punchline: Marge treats fish heads as normal food Bart needs to finish first
jokes.explanation: Nice callback that shows role reversal with typical maternal dinner enforcement.

jokes.text: That nerds conduct electricity. / Ow!
jokes.setup: Lisa asks what Bart's project proves
jokes.punchline: Bart's 'experiment' is just shocking Lisa with the premise that nerds conduct electricity
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart-Lisa dynamic with pseudo-scientific bullying disguised as experimentation.

jokes.text: Hey, these aren't waffles. These are just square pancakes.
jokes.setup: Family breakfast scene
jokes.punchline: Homer's technical but useless distinction about breakfast foods
jokes.explanation: Homer's pedantic food observation that's technically correct but misses the point.

jokes.text: That waffle iron's been in the shop forever.
jokes.setup: Marge apologizes for the waffle iron being in the shop
jokes.punchline: Homer's exaggerated complaint suggesting chronic appliance neglect
jokes.explanation: Minor character moment showing Homer's domestic complaints without much comedic impact.

jokes.text: [Gasps] I've created Lutherans.
jokes.setup: Lisa observes someone nailing something to cathedral door in her micro-civilization
jokes.punchline: She recognizes the historical reference and reacts with mock horror
jokes.explanation: Brilliant historical reference joke that's both educational and funny. Lisa's horror at creating a religious denomination is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Hey, you built a model city. Is that the school? Whoops, my finger slipped. Whoops, my finger slipped. Whoops, my finger slipped.
jokes.setup: Bart discovers Lisa's civilization
jokes.punchline: Pretends accidental destruction while clearly being deliberate and repetitive
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart behavior - gleeful destruction disguised as accidents. The repetition makes the 'accident' claim obviously false.

jokes.text: Sooner or later you'll let your guard down and then, flush, it's toilet time for Tiny Town.
jokes.setup: Bart threatens Lisa's civilization
jokes.punchline: Alliterative threat combining toilet humor with the tiny scale
jokes.explanation: Effective threat with good alliteration and scale-appropriate menace. Classic Bart antagonism.

jokes.text: It worked! The debigulator worked!
jokes.setup: Lisa appears to have shrunk into her civilization
jokes.punchline: Made-up scientific term 'debigulator' delivered with scientific triumph
jokes.explanation: Fun made-up word that sounds scientific. Lisa's excitement over successful miniaturization is in character.

jokes.text: We have listened to you speak since the dawn of time, O Creator, and we have learned to 'imitoot' you 'exortly.'
jokes.setup: Tiny people explain how they learned English
jokes.punchline: Mispronounce 'imitate' and 'exactly' while claiming perfect mimicry
jokes.explanation: Clever contradiction - claiming perfect imitation while demonstrating imperfect pronunciation. The made-up words are endearing.

jokes.text: The devil is your brother? [All gasp] I must say, we find that quite perplexing.
jokes.setup: Lisa explains Bart is just her brother, not the devil
jokes.punchline: Polite understatement about this theological complication
jokes.explanation: Polite theological confusion delivered with British understatement. The formality contrasts with the absurd situation.

jokes.text: If you're so good, why do you allow bad things to happen? Why am I so fat?
jokes.setup: Bill Watson poses classic theological questions to 'god' Lisa
jokes.punchline: Immediately personalizes cosmic question to his own weight issues
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from philosophical theodicy to petty personal concerns. Very relatable human behavior.

jokes.text: Un-shrink you? Well, that would require some sort of a 'rebigulator'... which is a concept so ridiculous it makes me want to laugh out loud and chortle
jokes.setup: Lisa asks to be returned to normal size
jokes.punchline: Leader invents 'rebigulator' then immediately mocks the concept's absurdity
jokes.explanation: Self-aware humor about made-up science words. The character recognizes the absurdity while participating in it.

jokes.text: Shouldn't you people be groveling? And bring me some shoes- nice ones. / She'll want socks too. I'll get socks.
jokes.setup: Lisa accepts her role as trapped deity
jokes.punchline: Immediately becomes demanding, with tiny people eagerly anticipating her needs
jokes.explanation: Lisa's character corruption arc completed with petty demands. The eager accommodation shows how power corrupts.

jokes.text: So peaceful and relaxing- doesn't even matter if I catch a single fish. Come on, you stupid fish. Take the bait! Don't make me come down there!
jokes.setup: Homer expresses zen-like fishing philosophy
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts himself with aggressive demands at fish
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - claims philosophical detachment then immediately proves the opposite. Great character consistency.

jokes.text: Don't eat me. I have a wife and kids. Eat them!
jokes.setup: Homer pleads for his life to aliens
jokes.punchline: Immediately offers family as alternative meal
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer selfishness taken to extreme. The casual family betrayal is shockingly funny and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Stop! We have reached the limits of what rectal probing can teach us.
jokes.setup: Homer assumes aliens want to probe him
jokes.punchline: Aliens have apparently exhausted the scientific value of this common alien trope
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of alien abduction clichés. Treats probing as legitimate but limited research methodology.

jokes.text: Bob Dole doesn't need this.
jokes.setup: Bob Dole is being kidnapped by aliens
jokes.punchline: Dole's characteristic third-person speech pattern maintained even during abduction
jokes.explanation: Perfect political impersonation - Dole's speech quirk persists even in alien crisis. Good character observation.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, Lyndon LaRouche was right!
jokes.setup: Homer witnesses alien conspiracy
jokes.punchline: Credits fringe political figure for being correct about aliens
jokes.explanation: Excellent deep cut political reference. The idea that LaRouche conspiracy theories would be validated is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Rum- so no one will believe your story!
jokes.setup: Homer asks what the aliens are spraying on him
jokes.punchline: Rum to discredit him rather than harm him
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - expects alien weapon but gets practical discrediting tool. Very efficient and clever.

jokes.text: That's Slick Willie for you- always with the smooth talk.
jokes.setup: Fake Clinton makes threatening alien speech
jokes.punchline: Brockman treats obvious alien threat as typical political rhetoric
jokes.explanation: Great media satire - news anchor's inability to recognize obvious alien threat. Perfect Brockman obliviousness.

jokes.text: Well, a refreshingly frank response there from Senator Bob Dole.
jokes.setup: Fake Dole threatens planetary doom
jokes.punchline: Brockman treats apocalyptic threat as refreshing political honesty
jokes.explanation: Excellent media satire callback. Brockman's professional detachment from obvious crisis is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Abortions for all. [Crowd booing] Very well. No abortions for anyone. [Crowd booing] Hmm. Abortions for some, miniature American flags for others. [Crowd cheers]
jokes.setup: Alien tries to please crowd with opposing abortion stances
jokes.punchline: Nonsensical compromise combining abortion access with tiny flags satisfies everyone
jokes.explanation: Brilliant political satire about meaningless political compromises. The absurd solution that pleases everyone is comedy gold.

jokes.text: Fooling these Earth voters is easier than expected. Yes. All they want to hear are bland pleasantries embellished by an occasional saxophone solo or infant kiss.
jokes.setup: Aliens discuss their successful political deception
jokes.punchline: Accurately diagnose political theater requirements
jokes.explanation: Sharp political commentary disguised as alien observation. The specific references to Clinton's saxophone and baby-kissing are perfectly targeted.

jokes.text: We are merely exchanging long protein strings. If you can think of a simpler way, I'd like to hear it.
jokes.setup: Reporter questions why candidates are holding hands
jokes.punchline: Alien explains hand-holding in unnecessarily scientific terms while being defensive
jokes.explanation: Great alien perspective on human behavior. The defensive technical explanation for simple gesture is perfectly alien logic.

jokes.text: As a young boy, I dreamed of being a baseball. But tonight I say we must move forward, not backward, upward, not forward, and always twirling, twirling, twirling towards freedom!
jokes.setup: Fake Clinton gives campaign speech
jokes.punchline: Completely nonsensical autobiography and contradictory direction culminating in meaningless 'twirling' mantra
jokes.explanation: Masterpiece of political satire. Perfectly captures meaningless campaign rhetoric while being utterly absurd. 'Twirling towards freedom' is inspired nonsense.

jokes.text: I am so mad at the Secret Service right now.
jokes.setup: Real Clinton trapped naked in alien tube
jokes.punchline: Treats cosmic kidnapping as security service failure
jokes.explanation: Good understatement humor - treating alien abduction as professional disappointment rather than cosmic horror.

jokes.text: It's a two-party system. You have to vote for one of us.
jokes.setup: Aliens revealed as candidates
jokes.punchline: Alien correctly identifies electoral system limitations
jokes.explanation: Brilliant political commentary. Alien invasion becomes irrelevant because of electoral system constraints. Very quotable and insightful.

jokes.text: Well, I believe I'll vote for a third-party candidate. / Go ahead. Throw your vote away.
jokes.setup: Citizen suggests third-party vote as solution
jokes.punchline: Alien dismisses this with common third-party vote criticism
jokes.explanation: Perfect political satire. Even alien overlords use the same arguments against third parties. Extremely quotable and cuts to political reality.

jokes.text: Don't blame me. I voted for Kodos.
jokes.setup: Homer forced into alien labor camp
jokes.punchline: Takes no responsibility because he voted for the 'other' alien
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending line. Captures voter psychology perfectly - avoiding responsibility through meaningless choice between identical options. Became cultural touchstone.
